title: Malignant Hyperthermia
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Anesthesiology, Emergency Medicine, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize early signs of MH during anesthesia

vignette.text:
A 22-year-old man undergoes appendectomy under general anesthesia with sevoflurane and succinylcholine. Thirty minutes into surgery, the anesthesiologist notices increasing ETCO2, tachycardia, and the patient is becoming rigid despite adequate anesthesia.

Past Medical History: None known

Family History: Uncle died during surgery 'from a reaction'

physicalExam.exam: Masseter muscle rigidity, diffuse muscle rigidity, mottled skin

questions.stem: This patient has hypercarbia, tachycardia, rigidity, and rising temperature during anesthesia.
questions.question: What are the clinical features of malignant hyperthermia?

options.text: MH CLINICAL FEATURES: EARLY SIGNS: Unexplained tachycardia; Rising ETCO2 (earliest and most sensitive); Masseter muscle rigidity (after succinylcholine); Tachypnea (if spontaneous breathing); LATER SIGNS: Hyperthermia (may rise 1-2°C every 5 min); Generalized muscle rigidity; Mottled/cyanotic skin; Arrhythmias; LABORATORY: Hyperkalemia (rhabdomyolysis); Respiratory and metabolic acidosis; Elevated CK (markedly elevated); Myoglobinuria; DIC (late); TIMING: Can occur at any point during anesthesia; May occur in recovery room; May develop hours post-op; FAMILY HISTORY: Autosomal dominant; May have history of unexplained anesthetic deaths
MH only occurs immediately after induction

questions.explanation: MH Recognition: EARLY signs - unexplained tachycardia, rising ETCO2 (earliest/most sensitive), masseter rigidity after succinylcholine. LATER - hyperthermia (can rise 1-2°C/5 min), generalized rigidity, arrhythmias. LABS: Hyperkalemia, acidosis, very elevated CK, myoglobinuria. Can occur anytime during anesthesia or even in recovery. Family history of anesthetic deaths suggests autosomal dominant inheritance.

questions.stem: This patient received sevoflurane and succinylcholine.
questions.question: What triggers malignant hyperthermia?

options.text: MH TRIGGERS: VOLATILE ANESTHETICS (all): Sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane; Halothane (historic, most potent trigger); SUCCINYLCHOLINE: Depolarizing neuromuscular blocker; Can trigger alone or with volatiles; GENETIC BASIS: RYR1 gene mutation (most common); Ryanodine receptor dysfunction; Uncontrolled calcium release from SR; Autosomal dominant inheritance; SAFE AGENTS: Nitrous oxide; Propofol, etomidate, ketamine; Opioids; Benzodiazepines; Non-depolarizing NMB (rocuronium, vecuronium); Local anesthetics; NOT TRIGGERS (commonly confused): Stress, heat, exercise (in MH-susceptible); These trigger similar conditions but not true MH

questions.explanation: MH Triggers: ALL volatile anesthetics (sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, halothane) and SUCCINYLCHOLINE. Genetic basis: RYR1 mutations causing uncontrolled calcium release, autosomal dominant. SAFE agents: Nitrous oxide, propofol, ketamine, opioids, benzos, non-depolarizing NMB, local anesthetics. Heat/exercise don't trigger true MH (but can trigger related myopathies).

questions.question: What is the emergency treatment for MH?

options.text: MH EMERGENCY TREATMENT: IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: (1) STOP all triggers (turn off volatile, stop succinylcholine); (2) CALL FOR HELP - get MH cart/dantrolene; (3) Hyperventilate with 100% O2 (high flows 10+ L/min); (4) DANTROLENE: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus; Repeat every 5 min until symptoms resolve; May need 10+ mg/kg total; Continue 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h; (5) COOL PATIENT: Iced saline IV, ice packs, cooling blanket; Stop cooling at 38°C (avoid overshoot); (6) TREAT HYPERKALEMIA: Calcium, insulin/glucose, bicarbonate; (7) TREAT ARRHYTHMIAS: Avoid calcium channel blockers (interact with dantrolene); (8) Monitor UOP (myoglobinuria risk); (9) ICU admission mandatory

questions.explanation: MH Emergency Treatment: STOP triggers immediately. DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat q5min until resolution (may need 10+ mg/kg). Hyperventilate with 100% O2 at high flows. COOL patient (ice, cold saline). Treat hyperkalemia. AVOID calcium channel blockers (interact with dantrolene causing cardiovascular collapse). Mandatory ICU admission. Continue dantrolene 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h.

questions.question: What do you need to know about dantrolene?

options.text: DANTROLENE: MECHANISM: Direct-acting muscle relaxant; Blocks calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum; DOSING: Initial: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus; Repeat q5 min until symptoms resolve; Maximum in crisis: No absolute max - give until it works; Typical total: 10 mg/kg but may need more; Maintenance: 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h; PREPARATION: Reconstitute with sterile water (time-consuming); Each vial = 20mg (needs 60mL water); Newer formulation (Ryanodex) easier to mix; SIDE EFFECTS: Muscle weakness; Phlebitis; Hepatotoxicity (chronic use); CRITICAL: Do NOT give with calcium channel blockers (cardiovascular collapse)
Dantrolene is easy and quick to prepare

questions.explanation: Dantrolene: Blocks calcium release from SR. DOSING: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat q5min until resolution (no maximum in crisis - may need 10+ mg/kg). Maintenance 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h. PREPARATION is time-consuming (20mg/vial needs 60mL water), newer Ryanodex faster. CRITICAL: Do NOT combine with calcium channel blockers (causes cardiovascular collapse).

questions.question: What is the complete management?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE: Rising ETCO2 + tachycardia + rigidity + fever + family history = MH; (2) STOP ALL TRIGGERS immediately (sevoflurane off); (3) CALL FOR HELP - activate MH protocol; (4) HYPERVENTILATE 100% O2 high flow; (5) DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat q5min; (6) COOL aggressively (stop at 38°C); (7) TREAT HYPERKALEMIA (calcium, insulin/glucose, bicarb); (8) AVOID CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKERS; (9) Monitor for: Arrhythmias, myoglobinuria/AKI, DIC, recurrence; (10) ICU admission × 24-48h minimum; (11) Continue dantrolene 1 mg/kg q4-6h; (12) LATER: Genetic counseling, screen family, MH susceptibility testing for future procedures

questions.explanation: Complete MH Management: Recognize (ETCO2, tachycardia, rigidity, fever). STOP triggers, call for help, hyperventilate 100% O2. DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg repeat q5min until resolution. Cool aggressively (stop at 38°C). Treat hyperkalemia. AVOID CCBs. ICU × 24-48h with continued dantrolene. Monitor for complications. Later: genetic counseling, family screening, future anesthetic precautions.
